title: Steak and kidney pudding
subtitle: Beef and ox kidney in a rich gravy, sealed in suet pastry and steamed for four hours
excerpt: Diced chuck steak and ox kidney braised in a dark, Worcestershire-laced gravy, enclosed in a suet-crust pastry and steamed for four to five hours until the filling is deeply tender and the pastry has absorbed the cooking juices. This is the most British dish in this collection.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: beefsteak and kidney pudding, recipe 654. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: beef and kidney suet pudding with gravy.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The filling can be made a day ahead and refrigerated. Assemble the pudding and steam on the day.

content.text: Steak and kidney pudding is a different animal from steak and kidney pie. The suet crust is not a shell over a pre-cooked filling: it is steamed alongside the raw or barely-browned meat for four to five hours, absorbing the cooking juices until it becomes part of the dish rather than a container for it. The result is a pudding that collapses when you cut into it, releasing a dark, concentrated gravy.

Ox kidney is the right kidney here: it has a stronger flavour and a more robust texture than lamb kidney, and it holds up to the long cook without dissolving. Soak it in cold salted water for 30 minutes before using to reduce the sharpness. Chuck steak rather than braising steak: the grain is looser and becomes more yielding over five hours.

items.prepNote: soaked in cold salted water for 30 mins, trimmed, cut into 2 cm pieces

content.text: Combine the {{self-raising-flour}} of self-raising flour, a good pinch of salt, and the {{lard}} of shredded suet in a bowl. Add cold water, a tablespoon at a time, mixing with a knife until the dough comes together — usually around 150 to 180 ml. The dough should be soft and slightly sticky, not wet. Rest for 15 minutes in the fridge while you prepare the filling.

Season the {{beef-chuck}} of chuck and the {{ox-kidney}} of kidney with salt and pepper. Toss in the {{plain-flour}} of plain flour until lightly coated. Combine the floured meat, {{onion}} onion, {{worcestershire-sauce}} of Worcestershire sauce, and {{stock-beef}} of beef stock in a bowl. The filling goes in raw — do not brown the meat first.

Butter a 1.2-litre pudding basin. Roll out two-thirds of the pastry on a floured surface to a thickness of about 8 mm. Use it to line the basin, pressing it firmly into the sides and leaving a 2 cm overhang at the rim. Pack the filling into the pastry-lined basin. Roll out the remaining third of the pastry to form a lid. Lay it over the filling, press the edges together, and fold the overhang over the top to seal. Lay a sheet of buttered greaseproof paper over the top, followed by a sheet of foil, both pleated in the centre to allow for expansion. Tie securely under the rim with string.

Place the basin on a trivet or an upturned saucer in a large saucepan. Add enough boiling water to come halfway up the sides of the basin. Cover with a tight lid and steam over a medium-low heat for 4 to 5 hours, checking the water level every 45 minutes and topping up with boiling water as needed. The pudding is done when the pastry is firm and has pulled away slightly from the sides of the basin. Invert onto a serving plate at the table.

items.cause: Too much water in the dough, or overworked
items.fix: Add the water a tablespoon at a time; the dough should be soft but not sticky; mix with a knife, not your hands, which warm the fat

items.symptom: Filling is dry with little gravy
items.cause: Too little stock, or the seal was not tight and steam escaped
items.fix: Use at least 200 ml of stock; seal the foil and greaseproof paper tightly; keep the water at a steady simmer rather than a rolling boil

items.symptom: Pastry lid has stuck to the paper
items.fix: Always butter the greaseproof paper that rests against the pastry lid

items.symptom: Water boiled dry in the pan
items.cause: Too high a heat, or forgot to check
items.fix: A steady simmer (not a boil) and a tight lid is the safest setup; check every 45 minutes without fail

content.text: Steak and kidney pudding is one of the oldest distinctively British dishes still cooked in quantity, with versions documented from the early nineteenth century in both Mrs Beeton and Eliza Acton. The suet crust steamed over raw filling is the definitive form; the baked pie with pre-cooked filling is a faster, lighter cousin. Rules of the Savoy made the pudding famous in the early twentieth century as a dish that could be carried to the table still sealed in its basin, the steam rising when the crust was pierced. The drama is part of the point.
